title: Hawking--Page Universality, Thermodynamic Dipoles and Categorical Defects
abstract: We reconsider the Hawking--Page transition using the common thermodynamic vector field whose zeros include the Davies and Hawking--Page points. In the elementary AdS branch their winding numbers are $w_{\rm D}=-1$ and $w_{\rm HP}=+1$, so the pair has zero total charge but a non-zero signed first moment. After normalization by the Davies scales this moment gives the familiar universal ratios $C_S$ and $C_T$; in four dimensions $C_S=2$ and $C_T=2/\sqrt{3}-1$. We check the construction for Schwarzschild--AdS, grand-canonical Reissner--Nordström--AdS, charged non-rotating black holes in arbitrary dimension, and Kerr--AdS at fixed angular velocity. The same reduced geometry gives a barrier $B=1/3$ in four dimensions and $B(d)=1/[(d-1)(d-3)]$. Finally we propose a formulation involving a defect-resolved version for categorical or non-invertible symmetry sectors.
summary: This work unifies the understanding of black hole phase transitions (Hawking–Page and Davies) by mapping them onto a topological dipole structure defined by winding numbers. The authors derive universal constants for these ratios across various dimensions and geometries. They further advance this framework by incorporating categorical defects, suggesting a deeper connection between gravity's thermodynamics and abstract algebraic structures.
main_problem: The paper reconsiders the Hawking–Page phase transition by analyzing a common thermodynamic vector field whose zeros include both Davies and Hawking–Page critical points.
main_result: It establishes that these transitions can be characterized by universal ratios ($C_S, C_T$) derived from topological charges (winding numbers) associated with the dipole structure of the free energy landscape. Furthermore, it extends this analysis to incorporate categorical defects.
method: The authors use a thermodynamic vector field approach to calculate winding numbers and signed first moments at critical points on black hole phase diagrams. They then normalize these moments to derive universal ratios independent of specific scaling parameters.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the thermodynamics of Anti-de Sitter (AdS) black holes, including Schwarzschild–AdS, Reissner–Nordström–AdS, and Kerr–AdS geometries in arbitrary dimensions. The analysis is performed using the on-shell free energy $F(S; Y_i)$.
key_observables: Universal ratios ($C_S, C_T$), topological charges (winding numbers $w_D=-1, w_{HP}=+1$), and the dimensionless barrier constant $B$.
important_parameters: Dimensionality ($d$), angular velocity ($\Omega$), and defect parameters associated with non-invertible symmetries.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that universal ratios can be extracted by normalizing thermodynamic quantities based on a shared functional form (shape argument). Extensions involve assuming control over both black hole saddles and defect sectors for full analytic treatment.
figures_summary: Figures schematically illustrate the phase diagrams, showing the locations of the Davies (D) and Hawking–Page (HP) points, and demonstrate how higher moments can distinguish between different configurations sharing the same total charge and first moment.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by defining the common thermodynamic vector field, calculating topological charges for simple AdS black holes to find universal ratios ($C_S, C_T$), generalizing these findings to arbitrary dimensions and rotation (Kerr-AdS), and finally proposing an extension using defect-resolved free energies within a categorical framework.
why_interesting: While primarily in gravity/QFT, the rigorous application of topological invariants (winding numbers) to characterize phase transitions and universal ratios echoes methods used in analyzing critical points in condensed matter systems, particularly those involving symmetry breaking or emergent order parameters.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Reduced Schwarzschild–AdS phase schematic portrait (adapted from [12]). Left: the Davies point D is the minimum of T(S) and separates the small and large branches. Right: the same point is the maximum of F(S), while HP is the zero of F on the stable large-black-hole branch. Dashed portions denote locally unstable states.
Figure 2. Normalized common vector field for Schwarzschild–AdS in the (r+, θ) plane. The zero at r+ = 1/ √
Figure 3. Four-dimensional Schwarzschild–AdS branch plotted as T(S) and F(S). The oriented separation from D to HP gives the two dipole components, PS = SHP −SD and PT = THP −TD.

Figure 5. Normalized Hawking–Page barrier. In four dimensions the Davies point is the top of the free-energy barrier, giving B = F(SD)/(TDSD) = 1/3. In the charged non-rotating family the higher-dimensional result is B(d) = 1/[(d −1)(d −3)].

Figure 6. Toy defect configurations illustrating the need for higher moments. The two panels have the same total winding number and the same normalized first moment, but their second moments differ. The dashed line marks the negative-charge centroid X−.
